title: Date and time should be avoided as a type for primary keys
description: Using temporal types as the primary key of a table is risky. When these types are used as primary keys, it usually means that each new key is created with the use of .Now or .UtcNow properties from DateTime and DateTimeOffset classes. In those cases, duplicate keys exceptions may occur in many ways: when entries are added consecutively by a machine with low-enough system clock resolution; when two different threads are inserting entries in close enough sequence for both to have the same time; when changes such as daylight saving time (DST) transitions occur, where values can be repeated the following hour (only for DateTime type); The rule raises an issue if: Entity Framework, or Entity Framework Core dependencies are found; a class contains a property either named Id, <type name>Id or decorated by the [Key] or [PrimaryKey] attribute.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Detect DateTime/DateTimeOffset properties used as primary keys
logic.triggering_conditions: (Property type is DateTime OR DateTimeOffset) AND (has [Key] OR [PrimaryKey] attribute OR named Id OR named <ClassName>Id)
never_trigger_on: Properties named CreatedAt, UpdatedAt, Timestamp, StartTime, EndTime (unless marked [Key])
logic.reasoning: Temporal types as primary keys cause duplicate key exceptions when using .Now/.UtcNow. Detects properties with DateTime/DateTimeOffset type that are marked with [Key]/[PrimaryKey] or follow primary key naming conventions (Id, TypeId). Excludes timestamp properties not used as keys.

checks.message: Date/time should not be used as primary key type (marked with [Key] or [PrimaryKey])
Date/time should not be used as primary key type (naming convention suggests primary key)
